content:
# Acts Chapter 16 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 245-246 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_016_page_001.jpg, ACT_016_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And he came to Derbe and to Lystra. And behold, a certain disciple was there named Timothy, the son of a Jewish woman who believed, but his father was a Greek.

**2** He was well spoken of by the brothers in Lystra and Iconium.

**3** Paul wanted this one to go forth with him, and he took him and circumcised him on account of the Jews who were in those places, for they all knew that his father was a Greek.

**4** And as they passed through the cities, they delivered to them the decrees to keep that had been decided upon by the apostles and elders in Jerusalem.

**5** So the churches were being strengthened in the faith and were increasing in number daily.

**6** And they passed through the Phrygian and Galatian region, having been forbidden by the Holy Spirit to speak the word in Asia.

**7** And when they came to Mysia, they attempted to go into Bithynia, and the Spirit of Jesus did not permit them.

**8** So passing by Mysia, they came down to Troas.

**9** And a vision appeared to Paul during the night: a certain man of Macedonia was standing and urging him, saying, "Come over to Macedonia and help us."

**10** And when he saw the vision, immediately we sought to go forth into Macedonia, concluding that God had called us to proclaim the good news to them.

**11** Therefore, putting out to sea from Troas, we ran a straight course to Samothrace, and on the following day to Neapolis,

**12** and from there to Philippi, which is a leading city of the district of Macedonia, a colony. And we were staying in this city for some days.

**13** And on the Sabbath day we went outside the gate beside a river, where we supposed there was a place of prayer, and we sat down and spoke to the women who had gathered together.

**14** And a certain woman named Lydia, a seller of purple from the city of Thyatira, a worshiper of God, was listening, whose heart the Lord opened to give heed to the things being spoken by Paul.

**15** And when she was baptized, and her household, she urged us, saying, "If you have judged me to be faithful to the Lord, come into my house and stay." And she prevailed upon us.

**16** And it happened as we were going to the place of prayer, a certain slave girl having a spirit of Python met us, who brought much profit to her masters by divining.

**17** She followed after Paul and us, crying out, saying, "These men are servants of the Most High God, who proclaim to you a way of salvation."

**18** And this she kept doing for many days. But Paul, having become greatly troubled, turned and said to the spirit, "I command you in the name of Jesus Christ to come out of her." And it came out that very hour.

**19** And when her masters saw that the hope of their profit was gone, they seized Paul and Silas and dragged them into the marketplace before the rulers.

**20** And having brought them to the magistrates, they said, "These men, being Jews, are greatly disturbing our city,

**21** and they proclaim customs which are not lawful for us to receive or to practice, being Romans."

**22** And the crowd rose up together against them, and the magistrates, having torn their garments off them, ordered them to be beaten with rods.

**23** And when they had laid many blows upon them, they threw them into prison, charging the jailer to keep them securely.

**24** Who, having received such a charge, threw them into the inner prison and fastened their feet in the stocks.

**25** And about midnight Paul and Silas were praying and singing hymns to God, and the prisoners were listening to them.

**26** And suddenly there was a great earthquake, so that the foundations of the prison were shaken. And immediately all the doors were opened, and the bonds of all were unfastened.

**27** And the jailer, having been awakened and seeing the doors of the prison opened, drew his sword and was about to kill himself, supposing the prisoners had escaped.

**28** But Paul called out with a loud voice, saying, "Do no harm to yourself, for we are all here."

**29** And having called for lights, he rushed in and, trembling with fear, fell down before Paul and Silas.

**30** And he brought them outside and said, "Sirs, what must I do to be saved?"

**31** And they said, "Believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved, you and your household."

**32** And they spoke the word of the Lord to him, together with all who were in his house.

**33** And he took them in that hour of the night and washed their wounds, and he was baptized at once, he and all his household.

**34** And he brought them up into his house and set a table before them, and he rejoiced greatly with his entire household, having believed in God.

**35** And when day came, the magistrates sent the lictors, saying, "Release those men."

**36** And the jailer reported these words to Paul: "The magistrates have sent word that you should be released. Now therefore come out and go in peace."

**37** But Paul said to them, "They beat us publicly, uncondemned, men who are Romans, and threw us into prison. And now they cast us out secretly? No indeed! But let them come themselves and lead us out."

**38** And the lictors reported these words to the magistrates, and they were afraid when they heard that they were Romans.

**39** And they came and appealed to them, and having led them out, they asked them to depart from the city.

**40** And they went out of the prison and entered the house of Lydia, and when they had seen the brothers, they encouraged them and departed.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Acts 16 is one of the great pivotal chapters in the New Testament. It narrates the decisive westward turn of the gospel from Asia Minor into Europe, propelled by divine obstruction (vv. 6-7) and a vision (v. 9). The chapter opens with the recruitment of Timothy (vv. 1-5), moves through the Macedonian call (vv. 6-10), and then unfolds three dramatic scenes in Philippi: the conversion of Lydia (vv. 13-15), the exorcism of the slave girl and its legal consequences (vv. 16-24), and the conversion of the Philippian jailer (vv. 25-34). The chapter closes with Paul's assertion of Roman citizenship and his insistence on a public apology from the magistrates (vv. 35-40). The "we" narrative, beginning at v. 10, signals the author's personal participation in the journey and gives these passages an immediacy absent from the surrounding third-person narrative.

**Manuscript condition:** The text of Acts 16 begins on page 245 (image 001), continuing from chapter 15 in the preceding columns. The column markers at the bottom of the page indicate Acts 16:3, 16:11, 16:17, and 16:23, confirming the column boundaries. The text continues onto page 246 (image 002), with markers at 16:31, 16:38, 17:5, and 17:11, showing that Acts 16 concludes in the second column. The text is well preserved with clear scriptio continua throughout. Standard nomina sacra are visible: IC/IY (Jesus), XC/XY (Christ), KC/KY (Lord), QC/QY (God), PNA (Spirit). The critical reading PNEUMA IHSOU ("Spirit of Jesus") in v. 7 is legible on page 245. The PUQWNA ("Python") reading in v. 16 is preserved clearly. The anarthrous ODON SWTHRIAS ("a way of salvation") in v. 17 is confirmed by the manuscript.

**Agreement among translators:** The three translations show strong agreement across the chapter. All three correctly preserve the "Spirit of Jesus" reading (v. 7), the "we" narrative shift (v. 10), the "spirit of Python" reference (v. 16), and the Roman citizenship assertion (v. 37). Primary points of divergence involve: (1) "spirit of Python" vs. "spirit of divination" (v. 16), (2) "a way of salvation" vs. "the way of salvation" vs. "the way to be saved" (v. 17), (3) "rod-bearers" vs. "lictors" vs. "officers" (v. 35), (4) the rendering of parabiazomai (v. 15), and (5) the degree of dynamism in the prison narrative (vv. 25-34).

**General translation approach:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS most closely in this chapter, as its formal equivalence approach balances accuracy with readability across the chapter's varied genres -- travel narrative, conversion account, legal proceeding, and dramatic prison scene. EMET's literal renderings are consulted for morphological precision, particularly in the participial constructions and the crucial textual variants. PNEUMA's dynamic readings inform the consensus in passages where English clarity requires departure from strict formal equivalence, though in this chapter PNEUMA's departures from the Greek are mostly expansive rather than substantive.

**Key consensus decisions:**

1. **Verse 6 -- "Phrygian and Galatian region" (ten Phrygian kai Galatiken choran):** All three translators treat this as two adjectives modifying one region. EMET and LOGOS render "the Phrygian and Galatian region"; PNEUMA renders "the region of Phrygia and Galatia," converting the adjectives to proper nouns. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "the Phrygian and Galatian region" preserves the Sinaiticus syntax, which has implications for the North vs. South Galatian debate.

2. **Verse 7 -- "the Spirit of Jesus" (to pneuma Iesou):** All three translators correctly render this distinctive Sinaiticus reading. This is a significant Christological formulation, distinguishing "the Spirit of Jesus" from "the Holy Spirit" (v. 6). The progression from "the Holy Spirit" forbidding (v. 6) to "the Spirit of Jesus" not permitting (v. 7) may reflect Luke's pneumatology. SOPHIA preserves the reading unanimously attested by all three translators.

3. **Verse 10 -- The "we" narrative shift:** All three translators correctly render the abrupt shift from third person ("he saw the vision") to first person plural ("we sought to go forth"). SOPHIA preserves this transition exactly. This is the first of the "we passages" in Acts (16:10-17; 20:5-15; 21:1-18; 27:1-28:16), traditionally understood as indicating the author's presence with Paul. The shift occurs immediately after the Macedonian vision, suggesting the author (traditionally Luke) joined Paul's company at Troas.

4. **Verse 12 -- "leading city of the district" (prote tes meridos):** EMET renders "first city of the district"; LOGOS renders "leading city of the district"; PNEUMA renders "a leading city of that district." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "a leading city of the district" -- the Greek prote here likely indicates civic prominence rather than strict administrative ranking. Philippi was not the capital of the first district of Macedonia (that was Amphipolis), but it was a city of primary importance as a Roman colony.

5. **Verse 13 -- "where we supposed there was a place of prayer":** EMET renders "where we supposed prayer to be"; LOGOS renders "where we supposed there was a place of prayer"; PNEUMA renders "where we expected to find a place of prayer." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: the proseuche could mean either the act of prayer or a place of prayer (a recognized term for a Jewish meeting place outside a city). LOGOS preserves this ambiguity-toward-place reading clearly.

6. **Verse 15 -- "she prevailed upon us" (parebiasato hemas):** EMET renders "she constrained us"; LOGOS renders "she prevailed upon us"; PNEUMA renders "she would not take no for an answer." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "she prevailed upon us" conveys the force of parabiazomai (to press, constrain, compel) without the archaic feel of "constrained" or the colloquial excess of PNEUMA's rendering. Lydia's hospitality is assertive, not merely polite.

7. **Verse 16 -- "spirit of Python" (pneuma Pythona):** EMET renders "spirit of Python"; LOGOS renders "spirit of divination"; PNEUMA renders "spirit of divination." SOPHIA renders "spirit of Python" -- follows EMET against LOGOS/PNEUMA. The Greek PNEUMA PUQWNA is visible in the manuscript and specifically names the Pythonic oracle spirit associated with Delphi. "Spirit of divination" is an interpretive gloss that loses the specific mythological reference. The Python spirit (associated with the oracle at Delphi) was a recognized category in the ancient world, and Luke's use of this term signals the specific cultural context of the encounter. See the Disagreement Log for full discussion.

8. **Verse 17 -- "a way of salvation" (hodon soterias):** EMET renders "a way of salvation"; LOGOS renders "the way of salvation"; PNEUMA renders "the way to be saved." SOPHIA renders "a way of salvation" -- follows EMET against LOGOS/PNEUMA. The Sinaiticus reading is anarthrous (without the definite article). This is theologically significant: the slave girl's declaration, while superficially accurate, is subtly distorted. "A way of salvation" (one among many) is not the same as "the way of salvation" (the exclusive claim of the gospel). Luke may be showing that the demon's testimony, while containing truth, frames the message in the pluralistic language of Greco-Roman religion. See the Disagreement Log for full discussion.

9. **Verse 28 -- "Do no harm to yourself":** EMET renders "Do no harm to yourself"; LOGOS renders "Do no harm to yourself"; PNEUMA renders "Don't harm yourself!" SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "Do no harm to yourself" -- the formal rendering preserves the gravity of the prohibition. PNEUMA's contraction ("Don't") slightly diminishes the solemnity.

10. **Verse 30 -- "Sirs" (kyrioi):** EMET renders "Lords"; LOGOS renders "Sirs"; PNEUMA renders "Sirs." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1): "Sirs" -- the jailer addresses them with the respectful kyrioi, which in this non-theological context means "sirs" or "masters" rather than the Christological "Lord." EMET's "Lords" could create confusion with the divine title.

11. **Verse 31 -- "the Lord Jesus" (ton kyrion Iesoun):** All three translators render "the Lord Jesus" without "Christ," correctly following the Sinaiticus reading against the Byzantine expansion to "the Lord Jesus Christ." SOPHIA preserves this shorter Alexandrian reading.

12. **Verse 33 -- "washed their wounds" (elausen apo ton plegon):** EMET renders "washed them from the wounds"; LOGOS renders "washed their wounds"; PNEUMA renders "washed their wounds." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "washed their wounds" -- the Greek literally says "washed from the wounds," but "washed their wounds" conveys the meaning naturally. The detail is deeply human: the jailer who hours earlier had locked them in stocks now tenderly washes the lash marks on their backs.

13. **Verse 34 -- "set a table before them" (paretheken trapezan):** EMET renders "set before them a table"; LOGOS renders "set a table before them"; PNEUMA renders "set a meal before them." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "set a table before them" -- the Greek paretheken trapezan is literally "placed a table beside them," and "set a table" preserves the concrete image of hospitality. PNEUMA's "set a meal" interprets the table as the food itself, which is reasonable but loses the physical gesture.

14. **Verse 35 -- "lictors" (rhabdouchous):** EMET renders "rod-bearers"; LOGOS renders "lictors"; PNEUMA renders "officers." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "lictors" -- the Greek rhabdouchoi specifically refers to the Roman lictors who attended the magistrates, carrying the fasces (bundles of rods symbolizing authority). "Rod-bearers" (EMET) is an accurate etymological rendering but unfamiliar; "officers" (PNEUMA) is too generic and loses the distinctly Roman administrative context. "Lictors" is the precise English equivalent and suits the civic setting of a Roman colony.

15. **Verse 37 -- Roman citizenship assertion:** All three translators preserve the emphatic word order of Paul's protest. The Greek stresses the contrasts: "publicly" vs. "secretly," "uncondemned" vs. "threw us into prison," "Romans" as the climactic revelation. EMET renders "uncondemned, men Romans being"; LOGOS renders "uncondemned, men who are Romans"; PNEUMA renders "without a trial, even though we are Roman citizens." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "uncondemned, men who are Romans" preserves the delayed disclosure of citizenship that creates the rhetorical force. PNEUMA's "Roman citizens" adds "citizens" (not in the Greek, which uses the ethnic/political adjective Romaious) and "without a trial" interprets akatakritous more loosely.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | "He arrived" vs. "And he came" vs. "Paul came" | "He-arrived" (katentesen) | "And he came" | "Paul came" | "And he came" -- follows LOGOS; preserves the conjunction kai and avoids naming Paul (the Greek uses the verb alone) |
| 6 | "hindered" vs. "forbidden" vs. "prevented" | "having-been-hindered" (koluthentes) | "having been forbidden" | "had prevented them" | "having been forbidden" -- follows LOGOS; koluo can mean hinder or forbid; "forbidden" captures the divine authority |
| 6 | "Phrygian and Galatian region" vs. "region of Phrygia and Galatia" | "the Phrygian and Galatian region" | "the Phrygian and Galatian region" | "the region of Phrygia and Galatia" | "the Phrygian and Galatian region" -- follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1); preserves adjectival syntax |
| 9 | "entreating" vs. "urging" vs. "pleading" | "entreating" (parakalon) | "urging" | "pleading" | "urging" -- follows LOGOS; parakaleo spans "entreat/urge/exhort"; "urging" captures the force without PNEUMA's desperation or EMET's archaic tone |
| 10 | "proclaim the good news" vs. "proclaim the gospel" vs. "bring them the good news" | "to-proclaim-the-good-news" (euangelisasthai) | "to proclaim the gospel" | "to bring them the good news" | "to proclaim the good news" -- composite; "proclaim" (LOGOS verb) + "good news" (EMET/PNEUMA noun); avoids the Latinate "gospel" while preserving the verbal force of euangelizo |
| 12 | "first city" vs. "leading city" vs. "a leading city" | "a first city of the district" | "a leading city of the district" | "a leading city of that district" | "a leading city of the district" -- follows LOGOS; prote indicates prominence, not administrative ranking |
| 13 | "sabbaths" vs. "Sabbath day" vs. "Sabbath" | "the day of the sabbaths" | "the Sabbath day" | "the Sabbath" | "the Sabbath day" -- follows LOGOS; ton sabbaton rendered naturally |
| 15 | "constrained us" vs. "prevailed upon us" vs. "would not take no for an answer" | "she-constrained us" (parebiasato) | "she prevailed upon us" | "she would not take no for an answer" | "she prevailed upon us" -- follows LOGOS; captures force of parabiazomai without archaism or colloquialism |
| **16** | **"spirit of Python" vs. "spirit of divination"** | "spirit of Python" (pneuma Pythona) | "spirit of divination" | "spirit of divination" | **"spirit of Python"** -- follows EMET (1-to-2 override); preserves the specific Greek mythological reference visible in the manuscript; see Disagreement Log detailed entry |
| **17** | **"a way of salvation" vs. "the way of salvation" vs. "the way to be saved"** | "a way of salvation" (anarthrous) | "the way of salvation" | "the way to be saved" | **"a way of salvation"** -- follows EMET (1-to-2 override); the anarthrous construction in Sinaiticus is theologically significant; see Disagreement Log detailed entry |
| 18 | "having been greatly troubled" vs. "having become greatly troubled" vs. "had had enough" | "having-been-greatly-troubled" (diaponetheis) | "having become greatly troubled" | "had had enough" | "having become greatly troubled" -- follows LOGOS; diaponeomai = deeply distressed/annoyed; PNEUMA's colloquialism loses the participial construction |
| 22 | "were commanding to beat with rods" vs. "ordered them to be beaten with rods" vs. "ordered them stripped and beaten with rods" | "were-commanding to-beat-with-rods" | "ordered them to be beaten with rods" | "ordered them stripped and beaten with rods" | "ordered them to be beaten with rods" -- follows LOGOS; the stripping is implied by "having torn their garments off them" earlier in the verse; PNEUMA's "stripped and beaten" is redundant |
| 26 | "loosened" vs. "unfastened" vs. "came loose" | "were-loosened" (anethē) | "were unfastened" | "came loose" | "were unfastened" -- follows LOGOS; anethē = were loosened/unfastened; PNEUMA's intransitive "came loose" loses the implied divine agency |
| 28 | "Do no harm" vs. "Don't harm yourself" | "Do no harm to yourself" | "Do no harm to yourself" | "Don't harm yourself!" | "Do no harm to yourself" -- follows EMET/LOGOS (2-to-1); preserves the formal gravity of the prohibition |
| 30 | "Lords" vs. "Sirs" | "Lords" (kyrioi) | "Sirs" | "Sirs" | "Sirs" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA (2-to-1); non-theological usage of kyrioi in this context |
| 32 | "together with all those in his house" vs. "to everyone in his house" | "together-with all the ones in the house of him" | "together with all who were in his house" | "to him and to everyone in his house" | "together with all who were in his house" -- follows LOGOS; syn + dative preserves the communal setting |
| 34 | "set before a table" vs. "set a table before" vs. "set a meal before" | "set before them a table" | "set a table before them" | "set a meal before them" | "set a table before them" -- follows LOGOS; preserves the physical gesture of hospitality |
| **35** | **"rod-bearers" vs. "lictors" vs. "officers"** | "rod-bearers" (rhabdouchous) | "lictors" | "officers" | **"lictors"** -- follows LOGOS; precise Roman administrative term appropriate to the colony setting; see Council Notes |
| 37 | "uncondemned, men Romans being" vs. "uncondemned, men who are Romans" vs. "without a trial...Roman citizens" | "uncondemned, men Romans being" | "uncondemned, men who are Romans" | "without a trial, even though we are Roman citizens" | "uncondemned, men who are Romans" -- follows LOGOS; preserves word order and delayed citizenship disclosure |
| 39 | "entreated" vs. "appealed to" vs. "apologized to" | "they-entreated them" (parekalesan) | "appealed to them" | "apologized to them" | "appealed to them" -- follows LOGOS; parakaleo here = appeal/entreat; PNEUMA's "apologized" interprets beyond the verb |
| 40 | "entered to Lydia" vs. "entered the house of Lydia" vs. "went to Lydia's house" | "they-entered to Lydia" | "entered the house of Lydia" | "went to Lydia's house" | "entered the house of Lydia" -- follows LOGOS; the Greek eiselthon pros ten Lydian implies entering her house; LOGOS makes explicit what is implicit |

-- SOPHIA
